onboarding.title: Welcome to ESPHome Learn 👋
onboarding.subtitle: You'll learn to build real smart devices across 22 guided levels. Here's the path:
onboarding.s1Desc: Drag-and-drop flows show how smart devices work — no code to start.
onboarding.s2Desc: See the real ESPHome YAML each block maps to.
onboarding.s3Desc: Write complete configs, then debug and connect to Home Assistant.
onboarding.s4Desc: Take your config to real hardware when you're ready.

hero.subtitle: Go from zero to real hardware. Start with drag-and-drop flows, discover the YAML behind them, then write complete configs to flash with ESPHome.

hero.subtitle: Learn ESPHome through 22 guided levels. Start by dragging visual flows, watch the YAML generate live, then export it to flash with ESPHome on real hardware.

features.title: Everything you need, in one place
features.visualDesc: Drag components onto the canvas, connect them with flows. No code needed to start — just intuition.
features.yamlDesc: Every node and connection you create instantly generates valid ESPHome YAML. Watch it build as you design.
features.hwDesc: Export your config and flash it with ESPHome — your device shows up in Home Assistant. From learning to production in minutes.

how.s2Desc: See how every visual block maps to real ESPHome YAML. Bridge the gap between concept and code.
how.s3Desc: Export your config and open it in ESPHome to flash your ESP32. Your device shows up in Home Assistant, ready to automate.

glossary.title: Glossary
glossary.subtitle: Plain-language definitions of the ESPHome and IoT terms you'll meet in the levels.

def.esphome: A system that turns a simple YAML config into firmware for ESP32/ESP8266 chips, so you can build smart devices without writing C++.
def.esp32: A small, cheap microcontroller with built-in WiFi and Bluetooth — the 'brain' of your smart device.
def.board: The specific dev board variant you're using (e.g. esp32-c6-devkitc-1). It tells ESPHome which chip and pin layout you have.
def.firmware: The compiled program that runs directly on the chip. ESPHome builds it from your YAML.
def.flashing: Writing firmware onto the device — done by the real ESPHome tool over USB the first time, then wirelessly after.
def.gpio: General-Purpose Input/Output — a numbered pin on the board you wire components to (e.g. GPIO5). Some are input-only.
def.yaml: The human-friendly text format ESPHome configs are written in. Indentation matters; tabs are not allowed.
def.component: A reusable building block in ESPHome (a sensor, light, switch, etc.) that you declare in YAML.
def.binarySensor: A component for on/off inputs like a button or motion sensor — it reports true or false.
def.sensor: A component that reports a numeric value, like temperature or humidity.
def.switchLight: Output components you can turn on and off (a relay, an LED, an RGB strip).
def.automation: A rule that reacts to something (a trigger) by doing an action — e.g. 'when the button is pressed, turn on the light'.
def.lambda: A snippet of C++ you can embed in YAML for custom logic ESPHome doesn't cover out of the box.
def.ota: Over-the-Air updates — pushing new firmware to a device over WiFi instead of a USB cable.
def.api: The native ESPHome connection that lets Home Assistant discover and control the device on your local network.
def.homeAssistant: A popular open-source smart-home hub. ESPHome devices show up there automatically as entities you can control and automate.
def.i2cSpi: Two common wiring protocols for connecting chips like sensors and displays using just a few shared pins.
def.pwm: Pulse-Width Modulation — rapidly switching a pin on/off to fake an analog value, used for LED brightness or motor speed.
def.pullResistor: A way to give an input pin a stable default value (high or low) so it doesn't 'float' and read randomly.
def.wifiCreds: The network name (SSID) and password your device uses to join your WiFi so it can be reached and updated.

header.title: What is a Smart Device?
header.subtitle: Understanding the fundamental pattern: inputs trigger actions, outputs respond.

demo.title: Interactive Demo
demo.desc: Click the button to see the basic smart device pattern in action.

explanation.title: Every smart device follows this pattern:

input.title: Input
input.desc: Something that triggers an action — buttons, sensors, switches, schedules.

logic.title: Logic
logic.desc: The "brain" that decides what happens when the input is triggered.

output.title: Output
output.desc: Something that responds — lights, relays, buzzers, displays.

challenge.title: Challenge
challenge.desc: Identify the input and output in each scenario.

header.title: The YAML Behind the Magic
header.subtitle: Every visual block has a text representation. Hover to explore the connection.

visual.title: Visual Flow
visual.desc: Hover over blocks to highlight their YAML

code.title: Generated YAML
code.desc: Hover over lines to see explanations

yaml.l2: A category for things that are either ON or OFF — like buttons, motion sensors, door contacts
yaml.l3: This sensor is connected directly to a pin on the board (GPIO = General Purpose Input/Output)
yaml.l4: Which physical pin on the board — GPIO4 is the 4th general-purpose pin
yaml.l5: The friendly name you'll see in Home Assistant
yaml.l6: What should happen when someone presses this button?
yaml.l7: Here comes the list of actions to perform...
yaml.l8: Turn on the light that has the ID 'my_light'
yaml.l11: A category for light components — LEDs, bulbs, RGB strips
yaml.l12: A simple on/off light (as opposed to dimmable)
yaml.l13: The friendly name you'll see in Home Assistant
yaml.l14: A short internal name so other parts of the config can reference this light
yaml.l15: Which output pin this light is connected to
yaml.l18: This output is a GPIO pin
yaml.l20: Internal ID referenced by the light above

header.title: Understanding the Structure
header.subtitle: Learn the vocabulary of ESPHome YAML by matching code to its meaning.

quiz.binary_sensor: A category for things that are either ON or OFF — like buttons, motion sensors, door contacts
quiz.platform_gpio: This component is connected directly to a GPIO pin on the board
quiz.pin: Which physical pin on the board this component uses
quiz.name: The friendly name you'll see in Home Assistant
quiz.on_press: What should happen when this button is pressed
quiz.then: Here comes the list of actions to perform
quiz.light_turn_on: Turn on the light that has the ID 'my_light'
quiz.id: A short internal name so other parts of the config can reference this component

header.title: Change the Behavior
header.subtitle: Switch from always-on to toggle — press once to turn on, press again to turn off.

explanation.title: Toggle vs Turn On
explanation.turnOn: always turns the light on regardless of its current state.
explanation.toggle: flips the state — if it's on, it turns off; if it's off, it turns on.

explanation.title: How delay works
explanation.part1: ESPHome actions run in sequence. By inserting
explanation.part4: , the device waits 5 seconds before executing the turn_off action.

edit.title: Insert the Delay

header.title: Conditions
header.subtitle: Learn how to add conditions so automations only run when it makes sense.

explanation.title: What is a condition?
explanation.body1: A condition lets your automation check the current state of your device before acting. For example: "only turn on the light if the door sensor is OFF (door is closed)." If the condition isn't met, the entire

example.title: Example: condition in action
example.description: The light only turns on when the door sensor is OFF
example.hint3: is OFF. If the door is open (sensor is ON), the condition fails and the light stays off.

quiz.heading: Quiz — answer all {n} correctly to complete

q1.question: Which YAML keyword do you use to add a condition to an ESPHome automation?
q1.explanation: ESPHome uses condition: inside an automation to check a state before running actions. It's different from many programming languages that use if.

q2.question: Where does the condition: block go in an ESPHome automation?

answers.a: At the top of the YAML file, before any components
answers.b: Inside the on_press block, alongside then:

q2.explanation: condition: sits inside the automation trigger (like on_press), at the same level as then:. The automation checks the condition first, then runs then: only if it passes.

q3.question: If a condition: evaluates to false, what happens to the actions in then:?

answers.a: They run anyway, but more slowly
answers.d: They run once and then stop

q3.explanation: When a condition is false, ESPHome skips the then: block completely. No actions are executed — it's as if the button press never triggered anything.

results.perfectBody: You understand ESPHome conditions. You're ready to build real projects!
results.retryBody: Review the explanations above and try again to get all {total} correct.

options.a: WiFi failed to connect to the network

result.correctAfter: means you tried to use GPIO34 as an output pin, but it only supports input. Use a different GPIO pin for your light.
result.wrongBody: Look at the red ERROR line in the log. The correct answer is:

header.title: Integration with Home Assistant
header.subtitle: The final step: connect your ESPHome device to Home Assistant for full smart home control.

apiBlock.title: The api: block
apiBlock.bodyAfter: block to your config enables the native ESPHome API. Home Assistant automatically discovers devices running this API on the local network and exposes their sensors and controls as HA entities.

configCard.title: Config with api: block
configCard.desc: The encryption key secures communication between the device and Home Assistant

keyTip.after: This encrypts all communication between your device and Home Assistant.

diagram.title: How it connects
diagram.desc: Your device communicates with Home Assistant over your local WiFi network
diagram.entitiesLabel: Entities that appear in Home Assistant:

celebration.title: Phase 5 Complete!
celebration.subtitle: You've mastered real hardware workflows:
celebration.next: Your ESPHome journey continues. Phase 6 covers advanced topics: lambdas, custom components, and I2C/SPI devices.

header.title: Lambdas
header.subtitle: Embed C++ logic directly in your YAML — the most powerful ESPHome feature.

steps.heading: Core concepts — click each to expand
steps.unlock: Read all {total} concepts to unlock the quiz ({read}/{total} read)

what.title: What is a Lambda?
what.explanation: A lambda is a block of C++ code embedded directly inside your ESPHome YAML. It lets you add custom logic that standard components can't express — conditional checks, math, multi-step reactions — without writing a full custom component.

syntax.title: Lambda Syntax
syntax.explanation: Single-line lambdas use single quotes. Multi-line lambdas use the YAML block scalar |-. Inside the lambda, the variable x holds the current sensor value when used in on_value triggers. Terminate statements with semicolons — it's C++.

id.title: Accessing Components with id()
id.explanation: You call any component defined in your config using id(component_id). From there you can read its state with .state, or call actions like .turn_on(), .turn_off(), .toggle(), and .publish_state(). The id must match the id: field of the target component.

example.title: Practical Example — Fan on Temperature
example.explanation: Here a sensor's on_value trigger fires every time a new reading arrives. The lambda checks the current value (x) and turns a fan on or off accordingly. This logic cannot be expressed in standard YAML automations alone.

quiz.heading: Quiz — answer both correctly to complete

q1.question: Which syntax correctly calls a component action inside a lambda?
q1.explanation: ESPHome uses id(component_id) to reference any component defined with an id: field. From there you can call actions like .turn_on() or read .state.

q2.question: In a lambda triggered by on_value:, what does the variable x hold?
q2.explanation: Inside an on_value lambda, x is automatically populated with the freshly received sensor value — the same value that triggered the callback.

result.successBody: You can now embed C++ logic directly in your configs.
result.retryBody: Review the explanations above and try again.

header.title: Custom Components
header.subtitle: Build your own ESPHome component in C++ and wire it into a YAML config.

info.title: How custom components work
info.desc: You write a {hFile} file containing a C++ class, reference it with {includes}, then instantiate it using {customComponent} and a short lambda. This lets you read any sensor or drive any hardware that ESPHome doesn't have a built-in platform for.

reference.title: Reference — my_sensor.h
reference.desc: The C++ class you'll be wiring up. Your YAML must include this file and instantiate it.

editor.title: Your YAML Config
editor.desc: Wire up my_sensor.h using the custom_component platform
editor.placeholder: Write the YAML to wire up my_sensor.h...

hint.title: Hint
hint.body: Use {customComponent} under a top-level sensor and instantiate with {lambda}

complete.title: Level complete!
complete.desc: You can wire up any C++ sensor to ESPHome.

header.title: I2C and SPI Devices
header.subtitle: Connect sensors and displays over the two most common hardware buses.

i2c.subtitle: Two-wire synchronous bus
i2c.example: BME280, SHT31, OLED displays, RTC modules

spi.subtitle: Four-wire synchronous bus
spi.example: Color TFT displays, SD cards, high-speed ADCs

spi.title: ILI9341 TFT over SPI
spi.desc: Color display with a custom lambda

quiz.heading: Quiz — answer all three to complete Phase 6

q1.question: Which two pins does I2C use?
q1.explanation: I2C uses SDA (Serial Data) and SCL (Serial Clock). On the ESP32, these are typically GPIO21 and GPIO22 by default, though any GPIO pair can be configured.

q2.question: Which ESPHome platform would you use for a BME280 sensor?
q2.explanation: ESPHome ships with a dedicated bme280_i2c platform. You just declare the i2c: bus, point the sensor at the right address, and ESPHome handles all the register reads automatically.

q3.question: What does the scan: true option do in the i2c: block?

answers.a: Enables encryption on the I2C bus
answers.b: Scans and logs all detected I2C addresses on boot
answers.c: Continuously polls sensors for new data

q3.explanation: With scan: true, ESPHome logs all I2C addresses it detects at boot. This is invaluable for debugging wiring — if your sensor doesn't appear, something is wrong with the physical connection or address.

outcome.title: Phase 6 Complete!
outcome.mastered: You've mastered the advanced ESPHome topics:
outcome.toolkit: You now have the full ESPHome toolkit — from your first visual flow all the way to custom C++ components. Go build something amazing.
outcome.reviewHint: Review the explanations above and try again.
